G04.design_md_evidence: Two places touch the territory but neither states the general per-sentence prune. §4: "Cut filler: 'leverage' as a verb → use; delete 'operationalize,' 'impactful,' and 'strategic' used as filler. … **Filler kill-list (delete on sight):** 'very,' 'really,' 'in order to' (→ to), 'the fact that.'" — these are FIXED substitution/deletion lists, the narrow instances. §11(C): "Edit in ordered passes … → (C) cut padding and tighten → …" — this is the closest match: it names a 'cut padding and tighten' editing pass, but as a one-line pass label with no method and no statement of the underlying principle. The general procedural rule (after writing each sentence, ask whether any word can be cut and, if so, cut it) and the Graham framing ('the best writing is what remains after you've cut everything that can be cut') do NOT appear anywhere in §1–12, 'What NOT to import,' or Provenance. The word 'tighten' is the only trace of the broad principle and it is unspecified.
G04.recommendation: Add the generating principle to §4 (it's pure Orwell #3, same canon the file already cites) as a sentence right before the kill-list, so the list reads as one instance of it. Suggested phrasing: "**Cut every cuttable word.** After writing a sentence, ask whether any word can be removed without losing meaning; if it can, cut it. The best version of a sentence is what remains after you've cut everything that can be cut. The filler kill-list below is the fixed, delete-on-sight subset of this; the habit is the broader rule and applies to words not on any list." Then optionally cross-reference it from §11(C): change "(C) cut padding and tighten" to "(C) cut padding and tighten (apply the per-sentence prune from §4)" so the editing pass points at the stated method rather than being a bare label.

G17.design_md_evidence:
COVERED at the ban level — §9 (Document structure), line 135: "Leading with the conclusion does **not** make readers leave — structure-first content gets read more. Don't *tease* ('the surprising reason this is mispriced') — that's a curiosity gap, not BLUF. When the facts are the value, present them plainly." The G17 source itself concedes this ("design.md §9 (anti-tease, partial)").

NOT COVERED — three refinements are absent. A whole-file grep for tease|curiosity|cliffhanger|open-loop|micro-gap|withhold|Zeigarnik|resumption|pull-forward|deck returns exactly ONE hit, the line above. So design.md contains: (a) NO safe-form carve-out — nothing says curiosity is permitted as a genuine question the very next sentences answer (an immediately-closed micro-gap); §9 reads as a flat, exceptionless ban, which over-prohibits given the cited Upworthy data (vagueness helped 8.7% of the time). (b) NO explicit no-cliffhanger / open-loop rule — the words "cliffhanger" and "open loop" appear zero times; the nearest line, §9's "Burying the point (the real point arrives in sentence three) is the most common failure," is about delay, not about deliberately leaving a loop open across a section/paragraph break. (c) NO "decks/pull-forward lines must preview a real answer, never tease" clause and no resumption-via-honest-re-entry framing.
G17.recommendation:
Add one clause to §9 (Document structure), appended to the existing anti-tease bullet, e.g.:

"Curiosity is allowed only in its safe form: a genuine question the next sentence or two actually answers (an immediately-closed micro-gap), never a withholding tease. Reject cliffhangers and open loops left hanging across a section or paragraph break — what readers actually resume is honest re-entry points, not artificial suspense. Any pull-forward line (a 'here's where this lands' preview) must state a real answer, never repeat the hook or tease the payoff."

This keeps the existing flat ban as the default and carves out the one narrow, evidence-supported exception, plus closes the cliffhanger hole the current text leaves open. Belongs in §9 because it is an opening/long-form-structure rule, alongside the BLUF and lede+nut-graf guidance already there.

G05.design_md_evidence: §4 has a written-filler kill-list but NOT a spoken-filler one: "Filler kill-list (delete on sight): 'very,' 'really,' 'in order to' (→ to), 'the fact that.'" — none of these are um/sort-of/you-know, and there is no "write like you speak, then tighten" framing. §11 carries only the inverse direction: "Read it aloud at speaking pace; rewrite anything that trips the tongue." §2 establishes the Boyle voice ("Calm, factual, dry, plainspoken... Humor, when any, comes from the fact itself, never from commentary winking at the reader... No 'this raises a profound question,' no manufactured drama.") but never says which of Boyle's spoken habits (conversational fillers, self-correcting asides) do NOT transfer to writing. §6 ("No hype") bans exclamation marks, superlatives, FOMO, "Confidence reads as a calm, plain statement, not a sales pitch" — adjacent to but not the same as casual bravado ("Let's get that out the way") as a tonal-inconsistency tell. No clause anywhere addresses conversational-but-tighter-than-speech, spoken-filler removal, or manufactured-casual-aside-in-a-formal-piece.
G05.recommendation: Add to §4 a spoken-filler line distinct from the existing written-filler list: "Conversational, not transcribed. Write the way you'd say it, then tighten past speech — cut the spoken fillers a transcript keeps: 'um,' 'sort of,' 'you know,' 'I mean,' 'kind of,' plus self-correcting asides ('well, actually...'). The Boyle deadpan voice (§2) is a spoken register on the page; its conversational hedges and mid-thought corrections do not survive the move to writing." And add to §6 (or §2) a tonal-consistency clause: "No manufactured casual bravado in an otherwise formal piece — a dropped-in chummy aside ('Let's get that out the way') reads as a tonal seam and is a tell. Pick one register and hold it."

G06.design_md_evidence: No audience model exists. §10 has the OPPOSITE-facing "Specialist-defensibility test. No sentence should read as wrong to a domain expert. Hedge or omit what you're not sure of." — that targets the expert reader for accuracy, not a non-specialist for accessibility. The anti-hedging half is present but scattered: §4 "Insincerity test: vague, hedged prose usually means the writer hasn't worked out their own meaning. Commit to the claim, or state the uncertainty plainly — don't blur it." and §8 "A plain 'we don't know yet' ... is more credible than hedging every sentence with may/could/potentially. Say the limit out loud." and §5 "No filler hedges/disclaimers." The translate-for-the-non-expert mechanism is present in §9 "Translate before you cite ... Untranslated jargon ('secular tailwind,' 'TAM expansion') gets translated or deleted, never used raw." and §9 "Never open with throat-clearing that announces the obvious." What is NOWHERE in the file: a named audience model ("write to a smart reader who lacks the terminology"), the words "condescension" / "trust the reader," "explain only the non-obvious," or the directional inversion "the fix for over-complexity is to simplify by trusting the reader more, not by adding hedging/apology."
G06.recommendation: Add a short audience-model rule to the §1–8 voice core (the natural home is right after §4's Insincerity test, or as a one-line §4b). Proposed text: "Audience: write to a smart reader who lacks the terminology. Explain a term only where the meaning genuinely needs it (per §9's translate-before-you-cite); never gloss the obvious — that reads as condescension. When an explanation feels too complex, the fix is to trust the reader more, not to pile on hedges, caveats, or apology. Cutting a qualifier usually clarifies; adding one usually muddies." This unifies the existing scattered pieces (§4 insincerity, §8 limits, §9 jargon-translation) under one stance and adds the two genuinely-missing items: the no-condescension floor and the "simplify by trusting, not hedging" inversion.

G02.design_md_evidence: COVERED (sales-pitch half): §6 "No hype — No superlatives ('best,' 'dominant,' '#1')... No FOMO, urgency, countdowns, or fake scarcity. Confidence reads as a calm, plain statement, not a sales pitch." §5 bans the exact SaaS-brochure verbs: "game-changing, supercharge, unleash, revolutionary." §2 "Descriptive, not rhetorical... let the reader draw the conclusion. No 'this raises a profound question,' no manufactured drama." §10 "Humor from logic... no wink at the reader." NOT COVERED (the two specific additions): (1) The 'committee-bland / Morningstar / characterless uniform third-person' anti-register is never named. §3 names uniform *sentence length* as an AI tell but that is a rhythm rule, not the characterless-committee voice. §2 prescribes the positive voice but does not flag the bland-committee failure mode as a thing to detect-and-avoid. (2) No concrete, portable 'brochure test.' §6 asserts the principle ('not a sales pitch') but the file contains no operational self-check phrased as 'if a copy line could appear in a brochure for a generic SaaS product, rewrite it.' Searched the whole file incl §9-12, 'What NOT to import,' and Provenance — no occurrence of 'brochure,' 'committee,' 'Morningstar,' 'pitch deck,' or a generic-SaaS test.
G02.recommendation: Add one sentence to §6 (No hype) as a portable self-check, e.g.: "Brochure test: if a line could sit unchanged in a brochure for a generic SaaS product, rewrite it — it is selling, not stating." Optionally add a half-sentence to §2 naming the opposite failure so the file brackets both registers: "Avoid two opposite failure modes — the sales-pitch voice (§6) and the characterless committee voice (uniform, third-person, no point of view); calm is not the same as bland." The sales-pitch ban itself is already covered by §6/§5, so only the brochure test and the committee-bland anti-register are net-new.

G10.design_md_evidence: §8 "Honesty about limits": "A plain 'we don't know yet,' or a clear statement of what the product does not do, is more credible than hedging every sentence with may/could/potentially. Say the limit out loud." — This establishes the BELIEF (disclose real limits, candor beats hedging) but gives no placement/framing procedure. The closest adjacent text is §10 "Anticipate and answer. Surface the reader's strongest implicit objection, then address it, rather than hoping they miss it." — this is objection-handling (refute a reader's counter), not caveat placement/framing, and it does not specify last/voluntary/lower-importance ordering. Also §4 "Insincerity test" and §5 "No filler hedges" reinforce candor-vs-hedging but again carry no placement rule. Nothing in §1–12 says caveats go LAST, read as VOLUNTARY, are framed LOWER-importance, follow a name-the-limit-then-name-the-guardrail (refutational) shape, or warns against engineering a token/fake flaw.
G10.recommendation: Extend §8 with a concrete placement/framing procedure (4 sub-rules + 1 guard). Suggested addition: "Place and frame caveats so they read as integrity, not hedging: (1) Put the caveat LAST, after the claim it qualifies — never lead with it. (2) Make it read as VOLUNTARY — disclosed because you chose to, not because someone forced it out. (3) Frame it as LOWER-importance than the claim it sits beside (a bounded qualifier, not a retraction). (4) Make it REFUTATIONAL where you can: name the limit, then name the guardrail — 'X is untested above N users; the queue caps load at N so it degrades, not breaks.' (5) Disclose only REAL limits. Never engineer a token or cosmetic flaw to seem candid — a manufactured weakness does not buy trust with a careful reader and reads as exactly the manipulation it imitates." Keep bounded-numeric phrasing consistent with §12 ("untested above N", not "may not scale").

G11.design_md_evidence: The nearest text is §8 "Honesty about limits": "A plain 'we don't know yet,' or a clear statement of what the product does not do, is more credible than hedging every sentence with may/could/potentially. Say the limit out loud." This covers only DISCLOSING a limit plainly vs. hedging — it says nothing about the VALENCE or FRAMING of that disclosure. Adjacent rules confirm the gap: §6 "No hype" ("Confidence reads as a calm, plain statement, not a sales pitch") governs tone but not disclose-as-strength; §4's insincerity test ("Commit to the claim, or state the uncertainty plainly") again addresses plainness, not positive framing; §10 "Name the incentive" is about explaining who benefits in causal claims, not framing one's own withheld output; §12 "Don't leak the plumbing" is the inverse concern (strip internal mechanism), not framing a deliberate withholding as a payoff. A case-insensitive scan for strength/apolog/self-deprecat/confess/deliberate/superior/credential/independ/valence/payoff returned NO matches across the whole file (incl. §§9-12, "What NOT to import," Provenance).
G11.recommendation: Add a sentence to §8 (or a new sub-bullet) capturing positive-valence framing, e.g.: "Frame a deliberate limit as a method, not an apology. When you withhold an output by design (a verdict, a number, a credential), state the payoff of withholding it ('we show the question the price is asking, not a fake answer') rather than confessing a shortcoming. Keep the voice non-self-deprecating: 'an independent research project,' not 'just a hobbyist.' A removed conflict of interest beats a disclosed one." This makes §8 cover both halves: say the limit out loud (existing) AND frame it as a deliberate strength (new).

G16.design_md_evidence: The core concept is already covered, but the quantified density threshold is not. §2 (Feynman rhythm): "Lead the reader *into* the specifics one beat at a time. Never open on a wall of numbers or claims; never stack three stats as the first clause." This IS the turtle-boil instruction (one beat at a time) and bans the wall of numbers, with one threshold (three stats / first clause). §9 (Translate before you cite): "Put the plain-language meaning before the number; numbers arrive in supporting clauses, never as the opening clause. Give every load-bearing number a scale anchor (\"$2B, about what the whole division earned last decade\")." — this covers "lead with the implication" + "brief framing for each number." §3 (Landing-sentence pacing): "Use one after any passage that stacks several numbers." What is genuinely absent: a quantified, countable threshold for a ratio-wall as a *density* pattern (5+ ratios in 2-3 sentences) that applies anywhere in the document, not just the opening. §2's threshold ("three stats as the first clause") is opening-specific; "wall of numbers" elsewhere is unquantified. The claim's own `why` field concedes this: design.md "bans 'a wall of numbers' ... but gives no quantified threshold."
G16.recommendation: Add one sharpening clause to §9 (or §2), not a new rule. Suggested wording for §9 after the "supporting clauses" sentence: "Watch ratio density: more than four or five ratios packed into two or three sentences is a 'ratio wall' a reader skips, even mid-document where the no-stats-in-the-first-clause rule doesn't reach. Break it up — give each load-bearing number its own beat and a one-phrase frame, rather than listing them." This (a) ports the quantified threshold the audited examples used, and (b) extends the existing opening-only threshold to mid-document density, which is the one thing §2/§9 don't currently reach.

G01.design_md_evidence: §10: "Humor from logic, rarely. If humor appears, let it come from walking an absurd premise to its flat conclusion — no wink at the reader. Budget about zero-to-one moments per piece." AND §2: "Humor, when any, comes from the fact itself, never from commentary winking at the reader."
G01.recommendation: The conceptual core is already covered: the 0-1 budget (§10), "no wink at the reader" (§2 and §10), and the deadpan reductio structure — "walking an absurd premise to its flat conclusion" (§10) — IS the premise-stated-conclusion-landed-flatly mechanic, and "comes from the fact itself, never from commentary" (§2) IS the rigor-not-commentary rule. The ONLY under-specified residual is the lexical-compression mechanic: that the joke is typically carried by a single word inside a sentence, with no constructed setup/punchline. If added at all, keep it to a clause appended to §10's humor bullet, e.g.: "...to its flat conclusion — usually one dry word inside the sentence does the work, never a built-up setup-and-punchline — no wink at the reader." Do not add a new bullet or restate the budget; that would be redundant.

G07.design_md_evidence: All five named ethos sources are already present, scattered across the file — the claim's own "why" concedes two; the other three are covered too. (1) Specificity: §12 "Every number is sourced. It traces to provided data, a cited source, or a defensible lookup," §9 "Translate before you cite," §2 "Descriptive… State the thing honestly." (2) Intellectual honesty: §8 "A plain 'we don't know yet,'… Say the limit out loud" + §10 "Anticipate and answer. Surface the reader's strongest implicit objection, then address it." (3) Technical accuracy: §10 "Specialist-defensibility test. No sentence should read as wrong to a domain expert," §11 "Edit in ordered passes — accuracy first." (4) Measured tone: §6 "No hype… No FOMO, urgency… Confidence reads as a calm, plain statement, not a sales pitch," §2 "no manufactured drama," §5 intensifier ban. (5) Authoritative calibration: §6 "Confidence reads as a calm, plain statement, not a sales pitch," §10 specialist-defensibility (hedge or omit what you're not sure of), §8. What is NOT stated explicitly: (a) the unifying "ethos / analytical-authority posture" frame that names these collectively; (b) the behavioral guard "do not swing to storytelling / keep the analytical posture." The closest is §2 "Descriptive, not rhetorical… No 'this raises a profound question,' no manufactured drama" and §10 "Humor from logic, rarely… Budget about zero-to-one moments per piece" — both adjacent but neither says "don't reframe an analytical piece as a narrative." Note §9's "Lede + nut-graf… sentence one is a concrete hook" actually permits a narrative opening, so the anti-storytelling-swing guard is not implied and is mildly in tension.
G07.recommendation: Do NOT add a new "ethos" section — all five sources are already covered, and a unifying frame is organizational, not a new rule (would duplicate §2/§6/§8/§10/§12). The one genuinely-absent behavioral rule worth a single line: add to §10 (Argument & rhetoric) — "Keep the analytical posture. State findings; don't dramatize them into a story ('let me tell you about…'). A narrative hook (§9 lede) is one sentence to enter on, not a frame for the whole piece — the deadpan analytical voice is the differentiation, not a limitation to write around." This closes the only real gap (the anti-storytelling-swing guard) without re-stating the five sources design.md already enforces.

G03.design_md_evidence:
Splitting the bundled claim by Orwell rule against the actual file:

RULE #1 (fresh metaphors / coin new images) — ALREADY COVERED. §5: "A fresh, concrete metaphor is welcome; a worn one is noise." plus the full banned-clichés/metaphors list ("headwinds, tailwinds, moving the needle, silver bullet... game-changing, supercharge, unleash, revolutionary..."). Both the negative (don't reuse stale metaphors) AND the positive ("a fresh, concrete metaphor is welcome") are present. The claim that "the 'coin new images' positive instruction is absent" is FALSE.

RULE #5 (foreign phrase / scientific / jargon word → everyday equivalent) — PARTIAL. §4 covers the short-word side (Orwell #2, not #5): "use (not utilize), buy (not acquire)..." and the Latinate carve-out: "Keep genuinely precise Latinate terms where they carry meaning — depreciation, amortization, consolidation — don't fake-simplify a technical term." §9 covers the jargon-translate side but ONLY in the long-form layer: "Untranslated jargon ('secular tailwind,' 'TAM expansion') gets translated or deleted, never used raw." So the jargon→plain principle exists, but (a) it is gated to long-form §9, not stated as a general all-copy voice rule, and (b) the "foreign phrase" and "scientific word" dimensions are never named.

RULE #6 (pragmatism override — break any rule sooner than say something barbarous) — REAL GAP. No general override exists. The only escape hatches are narrow and specific: §1 "Exceptions — leave the dash" (em-dash only, quotes/headings), §4 "Keep genuinely precise Latinate terms" (one targeted carve-out), and the intro "These rules layer on top of a project's own established voice; they do not override it" (project-voice deference, a different mechanism). The rules otherwise read as absolute. Provenance cites only "Orwell short-words" (#2). There is no "break any of these rules sooner than write something genuinely awkward" clause. This is genuinely absent.
G03.recommendation:
Two additions, not three. Skip #1 (covered by §5).

(a) For #6 (pragmatism override) — add a closing line to §8 or a short standalone clause after §8: "These rules are guidelines, not absolutes. If applying one would force a genuinely awkward, stilted, or unclear sentence, break the rule — clarity and natural cadence win. Orwell's own last rule: break any of these sooner than write something outright barbarous. The rules exist to catch lazy default phrasing, not to mandate worse prose."

(b) For #5 (foreign/scientific/jargon → everyday English) — promote the jargon principle out of long-form-only §9 into the general §4 voice rule, and name the foreign/scientific dimensions: add to §4 "Prefer everyday English over a foreign phrase, a scientific term, or in-group jargon whenever a plain equivalent carries the same meaning (per se → in itself, vis-à-vis → about, utilize → use). This is distinct from the Latinate-precision carve-out above: keep a technical term that has no plain equivalent; replace one used to sound authoritative." This makes #5 a general rule rather than a long-form-gated one.

G09.design_md_evidence: §10 covers the core move but not the refinement. Present: "**Anticipate and answer.** Surface the reader's strongest implicit objection, then address it, rather than hoping they miss it." (§10) and the section-mode frame "*Argumentative* sections (a bull/bear case): frame the thesis, then give evidence." (§9). Nearest to double-counting: "**Stay consistent across the document.** The same figure or claim must agree everywhere it appears." (§12). MISSING entirely: (a) the stronger refutational-two-sidedness finding that *conceding a genuine weakness in your own case* raises credibility (distinct from rebutting an external objection); (b) the "presumed biased" moderator — two-sidedness pays off most when the source is seen as partial; (c) the double-counting caution specifically as a rhetorical contradiction (crediting the same evidence to both bull and bear), which §12's numeric-consistency rule does not name.
G09.recommendation: Extend §10 "Anticipate and answer" with a concession clause and a caution. Suggested addition: "**Concede, don't just rebut.** Naming a real weakness in your own case (then explaining why the thesis still holds) beats only knocking down outside objections — disclosing a genuine flaw raises credibility, and it raises it most when the reader expects the source to be biased. **Caution against double-counting:** do not credit the same piece of evidence to both sides of an argument (e.g. the same fact cited as both a bull and a bear point); that reads as contradiction-within-document, not balance." Keep it short — one bullet under §10.
